title: A Practical Map of AI Chips: CPU, GPU, TPU, NPU, LPU, ASIC

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Do you like chips as much as I love them? Do you also feel lost sometimes hearing CPU, GPU, TPU, NPU, LPU, IPU? It's almost like an alien civilization sending us a signal: pew, pew, pew, pew.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I think it's time to draw a practical map and bring some clarity to the world of chips that can be as different as Etched with the model baked directly into silicon and the good old CPU whose lineage runs back to vacuum tubes.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So today let's zoom out. One episode, the whole map. By the end, you will know what each of these chips does, why it exists, and this is the very useful part, how to predict which one wins for a given workload. Because there is one rule underneath it all, and once you see the rule, the picture becomes clear.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Welcome to Attention Span by Turing Post. My name is Ksenia. Let's draw the chip map.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: One disclaimer before we start, because I know who watches this channel. What I'm about to draw is not a formal hardware taxonomy. Engineers will point out that CPU and GPU are processor architectures. ASIC and FPGA describe how a chip is implemented. And NPU is a marketing umbrella that means different things at different companies. They are right. What I offer is a lens, a spectrum of programmability versus specialization. And I'm using it because it happens to be the lens that predicts why these designs exist and which one wins where.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So here's the rule and I want you to hold this for the whole episode. The more general a chip is, the more flexible it is, and the more energy and time it spends on overhead for any single task. The more specialized the chip is, the faster and cheaper it runs one task and the more useless it becomes for everything else. So every chip in this episode is a point on this line.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Much of the recent history of AI hardware is the industry sliding to the right along it, one workload at a time. Let's start at the far left. The CPU has the oldest lineage here. It runs through early electronic computers like ENIAC, which was completed in 1945 and unveiled to the world in 1946 with nearly 18,000 vacuum tubes. It was programmed by physically rewiring it and setting switches. Hard work.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The idea of storing the program in the same memory as the data came along in Von Neumann's famous report on the EDVAC and that stored program architecture became the template for most computers since.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: In 1971, Intel's 4004 put an entire CPU onto a single chip and the modern era began. Pipelining, caches, speculative execution, multi-core designs, chiplets. Each of those were a real invention, not just polish. It was also the beginning of what we now...

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Silicon Valley. Yes, the name is thanks to chips and their silicon.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: What defines the CPU is the shape of its cores. They are relatively powerful, general-purpose cores optimized for latency-sensitive code: branching, deciding, jumping between tasks.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Depending on the system, that can mean a few cores in your laptop or nearly 200 in a modern server socket.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But the design philosophy is constant: make one thread of complicated logic run fast.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That philosophy is why the CPU orchestrates almost every computer you touch. It is also why it is a poor fit for training neural networks.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Because training is not one clever thread. Training is the same multiplication repeated trillions of times.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And asking a CPU to do that is asking a brilliant lawyer to hand-address a million mailing requests.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But hold on to the CPU. It leaves our AI story here, but will come back at the end in the wrap-up episode in a role few people expected.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Now to NVIDIA's most valuable creation: GPU.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Graphics processors existed through the '90s, but the modern category crystallized in 1999 when NVIDIA introduced the GeForce 256 and marketed it as the world's first GPU—a term the company defined and then made universal.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The GPU's original job was pixels, and pixels are a parallel problem. Every pixel on your screen can be computed independently at the same time.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So instead of a few powerful cores, a GPU carries thousands of lightweight ones.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And then came the discovery that shaped the last 15 years: neural networks are also a parallel problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: A model is mostly matrix multiplication, and matrix multiplication splits beautifully across thousands of cores.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The other half of NVIDIA's win was software: CUDA, the platform that opened GPUs to general-purpose programming, not just graphics.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The hardware generations you keep hearing about—V100, which introduced Tensor Cores in 2017, then A100 and H100, and now the Blackwell generation with the GB200 superchip—are that same parallel idea scaled relentlessly.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The GPU sits close to the flexible end of our axis. It can be programmed for a remarkably wide range of models and numerical workloads.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Whatever architecture researchers invent tomorrow morning, it will run it. That flexibility is exactly why it dominates. And, as we'll see, exactly what the specialist attacks.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Around 2013, Google looked at its projected demand for neural network inference and asked a heretical question: what if we stop being general at all?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The result was the TPU, Tensor Processing Unit, deployed inside Google's data centers in 2015 and revealed publicly in 2016.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It belongs to a category called ASIC: application-specific integrated circuits.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Application-specific integrated circuit, ASIC: a chip built for one job. The first TPU's job was inference, and its heart was a systolic array, a 256 by 256 grid of multiply-accumulate cells where data flows through like a wave, each cell doing a small piece of math and passing the result to its neighbor.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That data flow drastically reduced repeated memory accesses and control overhead, although the chip still kept model weights in off-chip memory.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: In Google's 2017 paper, the TPU delivered 30 to 80 times better performance per watt than the specific contemporary CPUs and GPUs it was benchmarked against—on inference workloads, of course.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Later TPU generations expanded to training as well. And that performance per watt number shows where the real enemy lives for a growing share of AI workloads.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It's usually not the math. Modern chips have compute to spare for many workloads, and especially for token-by-token inference, moving data between memory and processor becomes more limiting and more energy-hungry than the arithmetic itself.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Remember this, because nearly every chip on the right side of our map is a different attack on data movement.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The ASIC family has some spectacular members. Cerebras attacked movement by shrinking the distance. Their wafer-scale engine is a single chip the size of a dinner plate.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The WSE-3 carries four trillion transistors, 900,000 cores, and 44 gigabytes of memory living directly on the silicon. So most computation happens right where the data sits.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It doesn't abolish communication entirely, because big models still involve external memory and clusters of systems. But it collapses the distance inside the processor.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I did a full deep dive on Cerebras—you can read it on the link below. And since then, the company has gone public in one of the largest semiconductor IPOs on record. The wafer-scale bet found its buyers.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And then there is AWS Trainium and Inferentia. And as you can guess, Trainium is for training and Inferentia is for inference.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And notice what their story tells you: specialized silicon is not an inference-only story. A cloud provider designing its own training chips keeps the margin it used to pay Nvidia.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Then there is Groq's LPU. It was built for fast, predictable inference: deterministic execution, compiler control, weights served from on-chip SRAM.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That approach was effective enough that Nvidia licensed the technology in a deal reported at around 20 billion dollars, and much of the Groq team, including founder Jonathan Ross, joined Nvidia.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: As I covered in the GTC episode, the result is the LPX rack: 256 LPUs that now sit beside Rubin GPUs.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And the division of labor is more than LPU does decode, Rubin GPU does...

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: handle prefill and the attention computation over the accumulated context. The work that needs large memory. The LPUs accelerate the latency sensitive part of the decode loop. The feed forward networks and the mixture of experts execution served from SRAM at extreme bandwidth.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Two engines, one token loop. And at the absolute far end of the axis, Talos. The chip with the model physically hard-wired into the silicon.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Talos reports around 17,000 tokens per second per user for its first chip, running a heavily quantized Llama 3.1 8 billion with the company acknowledging some quality cost from that quantization. If you want a different base model, you manufacture a different chip. The design retains some flexibility through configurable context and LoRA fine-tuning, but the base model is frozen. That is the logical end point of specialization. The chip and the workload become the same object. You should watch our episode about Talos. It's very informative.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Notice what happened. Three of my previous episodes turned out to be points on this one line. The map was always there, we just needed to put it all together.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Most of the specialized systems we discussed are built for data centers. But a smaller class of AI accelerators already lives in consumer devices, including your phone. The NPU, neural processing unit, is a compact AI accelerator embedded inside a larger consumer chip.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Apple introduced its neural engine in the A11 Bionic in 2017, and every iPhone chip since has carried one. It runs Face ID and image processing on your device. Qualcomm's Hexagon NPU powers AI features in Snapdragon phones. Intel and AMD now build NPUs in laptop processors. AMD's latest reach around 55 trillion operations per second for on-device AI.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The design goal is not maximum speed, though the speed is huge. It is inference at minimal power, so your battery survives the intelligence. Consumer NPUs are optimized for efficient inference and are generally not used for full-scale training, and they can't replace your CPU or GPU for general work. Same rule, smaller scale.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Then there is APU. An APU answers a different question. Why choose? AMD fused CPU and GPU into a single package, sharing one pool of memory. The Instinct MI300A packs 24 Zen 4 CPU cores and 228 GPU compute units next to 128 gigabytes of unified HBM3.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: NVIDIA's Grace Hopper superchip follows a related idea, using NVLink-C2C to give the CPU and GPU coherent access to each other's memory. Data placement still requires care in both designs, but many of the explicit copies between processors and their overhead

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: disappear once again, the gain is in the movement.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Quickly I want to mention three more processing units you're going to encounter.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: IPU, Graphcore's intelligence processing unit. 1472 cores running nearly 9,000 threads with about 900 megabytes of memory distributed inside the processor itself. Built for fine grained, irregular graph shaped computation.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And then there is FPGA, a chip you can rewire after manufacture. For a mature fixed workload, an FPGA is generally less power and area efficient than a purpose built ASIC. But you can reconfigure it when your architecture changes, which in AI it does.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Then there is RPU, the research frontier, resistive memory that computes inside the memory area itself. If the enemy is data movement, the end game is a chip where the data does not move at all. IBM demonstrated a 64 core analog in memory inference chip back in 2023, and last year its researchers showed ReRAM device supporting on chip training as well.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Add processing in memory more broadly, neuromorphic chips that mimic spiking neurons and quantum processors, and you have the shelf of bets that could reshape this map in a decade or earlier.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So here's the whole picture and the rule that generates it. When the workload is still evolving, general purpose hardware usually has the advantage because it can adapt as requirements change. Once the workload becomes more predictable and runs at larger scale, specialized hardware can become worthwhile by reducing cost, latency and energy use.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Video decoding made this journey from software to dedicated silicon. Cryptography made it, Bitcoin mining made it in fast forward, AI is making it now in a more textured way.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Training still rewards programmability because models and techniques keep changing, but it already runs on both GPUs and specialized accelerators like TPUs and Trainium.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And inference where the economics are most brutal is where the sharpest new bets concentrate. Etched raised 169 million for model as a hardware, while MatX raised a half billion dollar Series B for chip designed to speed training, reinforcement learning, pre-fill and decode.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Different points on the axis, but same underlying calculation. This is also why Nvidia's own factory is no longer a pile of identical GPUs. The Vera-Rubin platform combines Rubin GPU racks for training, pre-fill and decode attention, and Vera CPU racks for the agent sandboxes, tools and reinforcement learning environments I covered in the last episode. Groq 3 LPX racks for the latency sensitive stages of decode and dedicated networking and context storage stitching it all together. The company that won the general purpose era is hedging itself across.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Across the entire axis, the GPU is not going anywhere, but the era when AI hardware meant one chip is closing and the era of the portfolio has begun.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: From now on, when a new three-letter chip appears in your feed, you don't need to memorize it. Ask yourself two questions: what workload is it specialized for? And is that workload stable enough to deserve its own silicon? The map will place it for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That's it for today. Tell me in the comments which chip on this map deserves a full episode. I'm watching the votes. And if a friend of yours is confused about all these pew-pew-pews, this is the video to send them. Thank you for watching and I see you next week.

highlights: The fundamental trade-off in hardware design is flexibility versus efficiency: general-purpose chips like CPUs offer maximum programmability but high overhead, while specialized chips like ASICs provide superior speed and lower costs for specific tasks.

The primary bottleneck in modern AI performance is often data movement rather than raw computation, leading to specialized designs that focus on minimizing the distance and energy required to transfer data between memory and the processor.

As AI workloads stabilize and scale, the industry is shifting from a general-purpose era dominated by GPUs to a "portfolio" approach that utilizes a diverse range of specialized silicon, such as LPUs for fast inference and NPUs for energy-efficient mobile processing.

The evolution of AI hardware follows a historical pattern where maturing technologies move from software-defined execution to dedicated silicon to meet the brutal economic demands of large-scale deployment.
summary:
The transcript provides a framework for understanding the complex landscape of modern AI hardware by placing various processors on a spectrum of **programmability versus specialization**. At one end is the CPU, a general-purpose processor optimized for complex, branching logic but inefficient for the repetitive math required by AI. In the middle sits the GPU, which achieved dominance by using thousands of lightweight cores to perform parallel matrix multiplications. While the GPU remains the industry standard due to its flexibility via software like CUDA, it carries overhead that more specialized hardware aims to eliminate.

As AI workloads stabilize, the industry is shifting toward specialized silicon known as Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs). This category includes Google’s TPU, designed for tensor processing, and Groq’s LPU, built for high-speed inference. These chips, along with radical designs like Cerebras’s wafer-scale engine and Talos’s hard-wired model-on-silicon, focus on defeating the "data movement" bottleneck by bringing memory and computation as close together as possible. Additionally, the transcript covers consumer-grade NPUs found in smartphones, which prioritize battery efficiency, and hybrid designs like APUs that fuse CPU and GPU capabilities to reduce data transfer delays.

The overarching theme is the transition from a one-chip-fits-all era to a "portfolio" approach. While general-purpose hardware like the GPU is preferred when research and model architectures are rapidly evolving, specialized hardware becomes the more economical choice once a workload becomes predictable and operates at scale. The transcript concludes that the future of AI infrastructure will be defined by a mix of hardware tailored to specific stages of the model lifecycle, with the winner of any given workload determined by the balance between flexibility and efficiency.
description:
CPU, GPU, TPU, NPU, APU, IPU, LPU, FPGA… all of these are AI chips! 
In this episode of Attention Span, we draw a practical map of them. One axis – from flexible chips to specialized ones – organizes every processor in AI.

You'll see where the CPU, the GPU, and Google's TPU sit, why Cerebras built a chip the size of a dinner plate, how Groq's LPU earned a place inside NVIDIA's own racks, why your iPhone has carried a Neural Engine since 2017, and what Taalas' model-baked-into-silicon chip tells us about where specialization ends.

More importantly, you'll learn the one rule that predicts which chip wins for which workload – the same rule that played out in video decoding, cryptography, and Bitcoin mining, and is now playing out across AI training and inference.

After this video, you will never be confused by a chip acronym again.

🔗 Links mentioned Full written guide: 
CPU vs GPU vs TPU vs NPU – https://www.turingpost.com/p/pu 
The Inference Chip Wars (MatX, Taalas, Vera Rubin) – https://www.turingpost.com/p/taalas 
Cerebras: an Engineering Marvel to Rival NVIDIA – https://www.turingpost.com/p/cerebras 
What is an LPU? – https://www.turingpost.com/p/fod41 
Previous episodes: Taalas HC1 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ibbB5CsDwxQ
 / GPU+LPU at GTC 2026 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QkJx025O-_g&t=1s / NVIDIA Vera https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IHfkTDMYa9g
Attention Span is here to show you AI isn't magic. Underneath every model, there is silicon making very specific tradeoffs.

👉 Subscribe for high-signal AI mechanics 👉 More analysis: TuringPost.com 👉 Interviews: @realturingpost

#AIHardware #GPU #TPU #NPU #CPU #NVIDIA #Cerebras #Groq #ASIC #Semiconductors #AttentionSpan #TuringPost #MachineLearning #AI
topics: The spectrum of chip design: programmability versus specialization
Comparative analysis of CPU and GPU architectures for AI
Specialized AI accelerators and ASICs including TPUs, LPUs, and Cerebras
Optimizing chip architecture for data movement and power efficiency
The industry shift from general-purpose chips to diverse hardware portfolios
